This classification is essential for maintaining a consistent and business-aligned IT landscape. Without a complete and validated capability map, it is difficult to analyze the IT landscape from a business perspective.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Tech Capability
CONTENT:
A Technical Capability represents a functional classification of IT components based on what the technology enables. It describes the core function or purpose of a technology, such as a database, operating system, or server software. Unlike rapidly evolving technologies, the technical capability model remains relatively stable over time and is industry-agnostic.
subject: Technical_Capability

• Only deployed software products need to be modeled manually.
• For SaaS products,
subject: Technical_Architecture

• Customers do not purchase isolated features or capabilities—they seek complete solutions that fulfill their desired outcomes.
• Digital Solutions enable organizations to align technical delivery with business strategy and customer needs.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Initiative
CONTENT:
An Initiative represents a targeted, time-bound effort that drives change within the organization. It requires dedicated resources, impacts the enterprise architecture, and contributes directly to the achievement of one or more defined objectives. Initiatives serve as the operational link between strategic intent and execution, enabling measurable progress toward business goals.
subject: Initiative

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Modeling Rule: When to Create a Separate IT Product
CONTENT:
Even if the same software is used across departments (e.g., CATIA, MATLAB), a separate IT Product must be modeled if: • It is used in a different business or logical context • It processes different types of data, possibly with different classifications • It involves different organizational responsibilities • It runs on different infrastructures (e.g., cloud vs. on-prem, Windows vs. Linux)
subject: IT_Product

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Differentiation from Business Processes
CONTENT:
Business capabilities and business processes serve distinct purposes: • Capabilities describe what the organization must be able to do. • Processes describe how those capabilities are executed. While overlaps may occur, the abstraction level and modeling intent are fundamentally different.
subject: Business_Process

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and Benefits of Business Process
CONTENT:
Modeling Strategic and Operational Insight Business process modeling is a key element of the business architecture. It helps identify: • Processes that are strategically or economically critical, • Processes that are under pressure to change due to transformation, regulation, or inefficiencies. This insight supports prioritization and investment decisions across business and IT.
subject: Business_Process_Modeling

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Business Capability
CONTENT:
A business capability defines what an organization must be able to do to fulfill its mission and deliver value. It represents a stable, organization-wide function that is independent of specific processes, systems, or organizational structures. Enterprise architects use business capabilities to: • Capture cross-functional needs across business units. • Provide a common language for strategic planning and transformation.
• Provide a common l

SECTION_TITLE: System Integration: LeanIX ↔ BIC
CONTENT:

For this reason, a synchronization interface is in place: Process Import from BIC to LeanIX • All active business processes modeled in BIC are automatically imported into LeanIX. • These processes are available in LeanIX for reference and linkage to other architectural elements (e.g., appl
subject: Business_Process

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: IT Component
CONTENT:
An IT Component is a foundational element within the technical architecture of an IT product. It operates independently of any business logic or context. IT components can either be: • Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) products acquired from external vendors, or • Custom-developed software created in-house. They serve as the technical building blocks that enable the functionality and operation of IT products.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Characteristics of an IT Product
CONTENT:
• It bundles business functions in a logically and functionally bounded way to process data.
• It may include custom-developed elements such as application code, configuration data, macros, etc.
• It can be a third-party software product, used as SaaS, cloud-based, or on-premises.
• It may represent a bundle of technical components that together provide infrastructure or business platforms.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Purpose and benefits
CONTENT:
IT components are essential to run, maintain, and change IT products. They may be: • Encapsulated within technical platforms, or • Directly integrated into the IT product. The use of IT components supports strategic architecture management by enabling: • Lifecycle planning of technical assets • Clear documentation of ownership, responsibilities, and competencies • Transparency in the technical landscape • Identification of redundancies (overengineerin
subject: IT_Component

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Linking Vendors to IT Components
CONTENT:
The most important relationship in a Vendor Fact Sheet is the link to the IT Components developed or provided by the vendor. These links are typically created during the modeling of the IT Component, not during vendor creation.

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Definition: Objective
CONTENT:
An Objective Fact Sheet is used to represent a strategic goal and the transformations required to achieve it. It serves as a formalized anchor for planning, tracking, and aligning enterprise-wide initiatives with business strategy. Objectives provide structure and traceability for transformation programs and enable consistent communication across business and IT stakeholders. Summary
Summary
subject: Objective

source_text_snippet:
SECTION_TITLE: Interfaces to External Systems
CONTENT:
In enterprise architecture, it is common for IT Products to interact with external systems— for example, to exchange data with banks, government agencies, or third-party services. These systems are considered external if they: • Are not part of the Porsche network (including ToGe networks), • Are not operated by PAG, a subsidiary, or a group brand, • Are not part of the internal Porsche IT landscape. Important: External systems must not be m
subject: IT_Product
